name: Ashton Place + Terrace View Portfolio

copy.tagline: A stabilized, cash-flowing 87-unit workforce housing portfolio along the I-40 corridor in Middle Tennessee — two newer-vintage assets (Ashton Place in Crossville and Terrace View Townhomes in Cookeville) acquired at roughly a 7.5% in-place cap with durable day-one income, modest capital needs, and measured rent-growth upside.
copy.thesis: Fireside Strategies is pursuing the acquisition of a fully stabilized, cash-flowing two-property workforce housing portfolio in Middle Tennessee — Ashton Place, a 64-unit community in Crossville (built 2001), and Terrace View Townhomes, a 23-unit all-3BR community in Cookeville (built 2000). Located roughly 40 minutes apart along the I-40 corridor and managed under one operator (Ambling), the assets share staffing and operating efficiencies while giving investors exposure to two of the fastest-growing secondary markets in the country. The thesis rests on strong in-place cash flow rather than heavy repositioning: acquired at an approximate 7.5% going-in cap, the portfolio throws off durable income from day one. Newer-vintage construction means limited deferred maintenance and modest capital needs — roughly $781K of targeted interior, exterior, and amenity upgrades (paint, appliance and cabinet packages, countertops, landscaping, a dog run, grilling areas, and parking-lot resealing) executed in place over the first 12–16 months to support measured rent growth without aggressive assumptions. In-place rents sit below comparable product across both markets, providing a conservative path to rent growth. Agency-style financing on Ashton (~6.36%, three years interest-only) and bank financing on Terrace View (~6.75%, one year interest-only) create immediate positive leverage against the going-in cap, supporting distributions from acquisition. The base case underwrites to roughly an 18% net IRR and a 2.64× equity multiple to investors over a seven-year hold, with an investor preferred return of 8% and a 70/30 LP/GP profit split up to a 2.5× equity multiple (50/50 thereafter).
copy.marketNotes: The portfolio sits in the Upper Cumberland region of Middle Tennessee, bisected by I-40 midway between Nashville and Knoxville. The Cookeville micropolitan area ranked among the fastest-growing micropolitans in the United States in 2025, and Putnam County posted its largest single-year population gain since 2006, driven primarily by net domestic in-migration and anchored by Tennessee Tech University. Crossville/Cumberland County continues to grow at roughly 1.3–1.4% annually. Both submarkets are dominated by small-scale (sub-50-unit) and older product, leaving limited institutional-quality competition and supporting pricing power for well-managed, newer-vintage workforce assets. The deal underwrites conservatively to ~3% rent growth and a 7.25% exit cap against an approximate 7.5% going-in cap.

marketLabel: Upper Cumberland (Cookeville–Crossville) Middle Tennessee multifamily submarket

marketFindings.headline: Cookeville Among the Fastest-Growing Micros in the U.S.
marketFindings.body: The Cookeville micropolitan area ranked fourth nationwide for numeric population growth among all U.S. micropolitan areas in 2025, and Putnam County recorded its largest single-year population increase since 2006 (~1,600 residents). Growth is driven primarily by net domestic in-migration and anchored by Tennessee Tech University — sustained household formation directly supporting workforce rental demand.

marketFindings.headline: Limited Institutional Supply in Both Submarkets
marketFindings.body: In Cookeville, large-scale apartment buildings (50+ units) represent only ~2% of the rental stock; roughly 73% of rentals are small sub-50-unit complexes. With most product older and fragmented, newer-vintage, professionally managed workforce assets like Ashton Place and Terrace View face limited direct competition — supporting occupancy and pricing power.

marketFindings.headline: Steady Rent Growth Across East/Middle Tennessee
marketFindings.body: Cookeville's average apartment rent rose ~1.9% year-over-year to ~$1,246, and broader East Tennessee rent growth is forecast to hold near 3.5% for 2025 — consistent with the portfolio's conservative ~3% rent-growth underwriting and supportive of the below-market in-place rents at both properties.

marketFindings.headline: Secondary-Market Liquidity and Exit Cap Risk
marketFindings.body: Upper Cumberland is a thin, secondary institutional market; buyer pools for stabilized workforce assets are shallower than in primary metros, and the underwriting assumes a 7.25% exit cap against a ~7.5% going-in cap. A softening in cap rates or rates staying higher for longer would pressure exit value — the single largest driver of the projected equity multiple. Stress-test the exit-cap lever before committing.
